title: A high-pressure profession, by its nature
paras: Planning is a high-pressure profession, by its very nature — so let me start there. Every planner, sooner or later, has one of those weeks. Three deadlines land together. A committee goes against a careful recommendation. A client is unhappy, and lets you know it. And an appeal decision arrives that you didn't expect. It's at moments like these that the difference shows — between the planners who last and enjoy the work, and those who are gradually ground down by it. And the difference is rarely talent. It's how they cope. Pressure isn't an occasional visitor to this profession; it's a permanent feature of it. The good news, learned over a long career, is that while you can't make the pressure go away, you can get much better at carrying it. And the habits that let you do so are simple, practical, and available to anyone.

It helps to start by accepting that planning is, and always will be, a demanding job. It runs on deadlines that don't move — determination dates, committee cycles, appeal timetables — and on decisions that carry real consequences, and often a great deal of money. It sits permanently between competing interests, so pleasing one party usually means disappointing another. And it's carried out in public, on the record. Add the breadth of the work, and the fact that most planners are busy, and pressure is simply the water this profession swims in. There's no version of a good planning job that's free of it.

Recognising that matters, because a good deal of unnecessary suffering comes from planners believing the pressure is a problem to be solved, rather than a condition to be managed. You won't reach a point where the work stops being demanding. That day never comes — for anyone. What changes over a career is your relationship with it. The experienced planner isn't under less strain than the anxious newcomer. They've simply learned to hold it more lightly, and to protect themselves, so it doesn't accumulate into something heavier. The first and most freeing lesson is to stop expecting the pressure to disappear, and start getting good at living with it.

A note from the file — the email I did not send. Years ago, I answered everything the moment it arrived, evenings and weekends included, and I was quietly proud of it. The lesson that broke the habit came the night I fired off a terse reply to a difficult client at ten o'clock — tired, and irritated — and spent the next morning undoing the damage. Now, whatever the hour, a message that arrives after I've stopped for the day waits until I have. Nothing has ever been made worse by a night's sleep. And a great deal has been made better.

title: Pressure you can ride, and stress that grinds
paras: Now — there's pressure you can ride, and there's stress that grinds. That's an important distinction to draw, because not all pressure is the same. Short bursts of it are normal, often useful, and nothing to fear. The push to finish a report for Thursday's committee. The focus a public inquiry demands. The adrenaline before a difficult meeting. These are temporary. They pass, and a career is full of them, without harm done. Most planners rather enjoy this kind of pressure once they're used to it. It sharpens the mind, and there's real satisfaction in rising to it — and then coming back down again afterwards. Pressure that peaks and releases is part of a working life well lived.

What deserves attention is the other kind — pressure that never lets up. The grinding sense of being permanently behind, month after month, with no recovery in between. It's worth learning to tell them apart. There are warning signs that busy has tipped into something more corrosive. Exhaustion that a weekend no longer fixes. Dread of the working day. The enjoyment quietly draining out of a job you used to like. Sleep going wrong. A shorter temper. Small mistakes creeping into work you'd normally get right. A hard fortnight is not this. A hard six months, with those signs settling in, is a signal to take seriously, and to act on. The skill isn't to avoid pressure — that's impossible. It's to notice the difference between the sort you ride, and the sort that's wearing you down.

title: Keeping a sense of proportion
paras: Keeping a sense of proportion. If there's one mental habit that protects a planner more than any other, it's that — and it's most needed exactly when it's hardest to find. A refusal at committee. A lost appeal. A scheme you championed, thrown out. In the moment, these can feel like disasters, and the mind runs to the worst of it — that you got it badly wrong, that your judgement is failing, that the client will leave. This is catastrophising, and it's almost always a liar. The most useful question to ask, in the low hours after a setback, is a simple one: will this matter in a year? Now and then the answer is yes — and then you learn from it. Far more often it's no, and naming that takes most of the sting out.

Perspective comes partly from experience. The hundredth refusal doesn't wound like the first, because you know from long practice that the work goes on, and that one decision rarely defines anything. But it can be practised deliberately, long before you have that much experience. When a setback hits, resist the urge to draw sweeping conclusions from a single event. A lost appeal is one inspector's view, on one scheme, on one day — not a verdict on your ability. And even excellent planners lose cases they should have won. Over a career of hundreds of decisions, the ones that felt catastrophic at the time are, looking back, mostly forgotten. Keeping that proportion while the disappointment is fresh is difficult. And it's exactly what stops a bad day from becoming a bad month.

title: Criticism, confrontation and letting it go
paras: Criticism, confrontation, and letting it go. Planning brings a steady supply of the first, and the occasional dose of the second. Much of the criticism isn't really personal, however personal it feels. The objector attacking your recommendation is defending their home. The member questioning you at committee is doing their job. The client complaining about a delay is anxious about money. Hearing these as comments on the situation, rather than judgements on your worth, takes most of their power away. There's a difference between caring about the quality of your advice and taking every disagreement as a personal blow — and the planners who last keep the two apart.

Confrontation is handled best by staying deliberately calm. When someone is aggressive, leave a short pause before you answer, rather than firing back. Keep your voice level. Stick to the planning issues, rather than being drawn onto personal ground. You can't control how the other person behaves — only whether you match it. And refusing to match it keeps a difficult exchange from escalating. Afterwards, close the encounter off. A word with a colleague, a short walk. And don't replay it at midnight — it will look far smaller in the morning.

title: Leaving work at work
paras: Leaving work at work. This is one of the most important coping skills, and one of the hardest to learn — the ability to leave the job behind at the end of the day. Planning is absorbing work, full of problems the mind chews over at all hours, and modern working makes it worse. The laptop on the kitchen table. The phone that carries email into the evening. The sense that you could always do a little more. But the planner who never switches off isn't more dedicated than the one who does. They're simply denying themselves the recovery that keeps judgement sharp and enjoyment alive — and heading, without noticing, towards being worn out.

Drawing a line takes deliberate effort, but the mechanics are straightforward. Decide when your working day ends and, most evenings, keep to it. Resist the after-hours inbox — it rarely achieves anything except turning a quiet evening into a worried one, over something that could have waited until morning. Find a ritual that marks the change from work to home. A walk, a change of clothes, shutting the study door. For many people, the commute did this without their realising — which is one reason home working can blur the line so badly. The unfinished report will still be there tomorrow, and you'll do it better rested. Protecting your own time isn't a lack of commitment. It's what makes a long commitment possible.

title: The unglamorous basics that actually work
paras: The unglamorous basics that actually work. Some of the most effective ways of coping with a demanding job are so ordinary that professionals are embarrassed to take them seriously — and they neglect them at their cost. Take your breaks. A short walk away from the desk at lunchtime improves the afternoon's work far more than eating a sandwich over the keyboard. Take your annual leave — all of it — in proper blocks, not odd days scattered when you're already desperate. Leave taken before you're exhausted is worth twice as much as leave taken after. The planners who wear their unused holiday as a badge of dedication are usually the ones closest to running out of steam.

Beyond that, the body and the mind are not separate systems, and looking after one protects the other. Regular exercise — of any kind you'll actually keep up — is one of the most reliable ways of managing pressure and clearing a cluttered head. Time spent outdoors does something for the spirits that no willpower at a desk can match: a walk in the fresh air, a site visit taken at a human pace rather than at a run. And sleep matters more than any of it. Planners underrate it badly. Tired judgement is poor judgement. The decisions and emails you regret are almost always made when exhausted, and a run of bad nights erodes clear thinking faster than almost anything else. The experienced planners who stay sharp for decades are, almost without exception, the ones who look after these unglamorous foundations.

title: Steadying yourself — attention and breathing
paras: Steadying yourself — attention, and breathing. Two simple techniques are worth explaining plainly, because they're genuinely useful, and often wrapped in language that puts practical people off. The first is mindfulness — which sounds mystical, and isn't. Stripped of the mystique, it's simply training your attention. Noticing when your mind has run off to tomorrow's committee while you're meant to be writing today's report, and gently bringing it back to the task in front of you. It's a skill like any other, improved by practice. A short spell each day of sitting quietly and paying attention to your own breathing, bringing your focus back each time it wanders, makes a real difference to how settled you feel and how well you concentrate. There are apps that guide it, but you need nothing but a few quiet minutes. And the benefit for a busy planner is down to earth: better focus, less mental clutter, and more distance between you and the churn of worry.

The second is even simpler, and it works in the moment. Before a difficult meeting, a committee appearance, or an awkward phone call, a few slow, deliberate breaths will steady the nerves noticeably. Breathe in slowly, hold for a second, let it out slowly — a handful of times. This isn't a trick, or a therapy. It's basic physiology — the same thing actors do in the wings, and surgeons do before an operation — and it works because slow breathing calms the body's alarm response. Give yourself a moment before you walk into the committee room, rather than rushing in flustered from the last thing, and you'll speak more steadily and think more clearly for it. Small, practical tools — not solutions to anything large. But on the day, they help.

title: A life outside planning
paras: A life outside planning. Perhaps the deepest source of perspective is the simplest: having a life that isn't about planning at all. The planner whose entire identity is bound up in the job has no shock absorber when it goes badly, because a bad week at work becomes a bad week at everything. The one who also has a family they enjoy, friends outside the profession, a sport or a hobby that absorbs them — that planner comes to the difficult Monday with ballast. A refusal stings less when it's one part of a full life, rather than the whole of it.

This is not an argument for caring less about the job. The best planners care a great deal. It's an argument for not letting the job be the only thing you care about. Protect the time for the people and interests that matter to you as fiercely as you protect a deadline, and hold on to the friendships that will still be there, whatever this week's appeal decides. The planners who stay balanced and keep enjoying the work are, almost always, the ones who were never only planners. A full life outside the office isn't a distraction from a good career. Very often, it's what makes one possible.

title: When you are no longer coping
paras: When you are no longer coping. For all these habits, there are times when the pressure genuinely gets on top of someone — and the most important thing this article can say is that recognising it, and speaking up, is a sign of good judgement, not of failure. Everyone, at every level of experience, has periods when they're not coping well. It's a normal part of a long working life — not a personal weakness, and not anything to be ashamed of. The signs are the ones described earlier. Exhaustion that rest doesn't fix. Lost enjoyment. Sleep and temper going. The sense of being permanently overwhelmed. When they settle in and stay, the responsible response is not to grit your teeth and push harder — which usually makes things worse — but to talk to someone.

Who you talk to matters less than that you do. A trusted colleague, a mentor or a manager can help you put things in proportion, lighten a load, or remind you that you're not the only one who has felt this way. And if the difficulty is persistent or serious, speaking to a healthcare professional — your G-P being the natural first step — is exactly the sensible thing to do. It's the same way you'd see a doctor about a persistent physical problem, rather than hoping it went away. There's no medal for struggling on alone, and much to be said for dealing with something early, while it's small. Asking for help isn't the end of a good career. Very often, it's what protects one. The planners who last are not the ones who never struggled, but the ones who, when they did, had the good sense to say so.

And if any of this is landing close to home, there's no threshold you must reach before reaching out. Samaritans are free, day or night — call one one six, one two three. Shout is a crisis text line, open twenty-four hours a day, seven days a week — text eight five, two five eight. Your G-P is the natural first step for anything persistent. And the full support directory is in the Where to Turn topic of Under Pressure.

title: Key messages
paras: So, to finish — the key messages. The habits and attitudes that keep a planner steady, gathered in one place.

First. Pressure cannot be eliminated, only managed. Planning is demanding by its nature, and always will be. What changes over a career is not the pressure but your relationship with it — stop waiting for it to disappear, and get good at carrying it. Second. Tell temporary pressure from chronic stress. Bursts that peak and release are normal, and even useful. Exhaustion a weekend cannot fix, lost enjoyment, and disrupted sleep settling in over months are a different thing — and a signal to act. Third. Keep a sense of proportion. After a refusal or a lost appeal, ask: will this matter in a year? Usually not. A single decision is one view, on one day — not a verdict on your ability. Fourth. Do not catastrophise. The mind runs to the worst after a setback, and it is almost always lying. Even excellent planners lose cases they should win; the process has real uncertainty built in. Fifth. Take criticism without absorbing it. Most of it is about the situation, not about you. Care about the quality of your advice, without taking every disagreement as a personal blow. Sixth. Stay calm in confrontation, then set it down. Pause before answering, keep your voice level, and stick to the planning issues. Afterwards, close the encounter off, rather than replaying it at midnight. Seventh. Leave work at work. Decide when the day ends, and keep to it. Resist the after-hours inbox. Find a ritual that marks the change from work to home. The unfinished report will wait, and you will do it better rested. Eighth. Respect the unglamorous basics. Take your breaks, and all of your annual leave, in proper blocks. Exercise in any way you will keep up. Get outdoors. And protect your sleep — tired judgement is poor judgement. Ninth. Use attention, and breathing. Mindfulness is simply training your attention back to the task — a few quiet minutes a day improves focus. And a few slow breaths before committee, or a hard meeting, genuinely steadies the nerves. Tenth. Have a life outside planning. Family, friends, a hobby or a sport are ballast when the work goes badly. Guard that time as fiercely as a deadline. The planners who stay balanced were never only planners. Eleventh. Resilience is built, not born. Everyone has periods of pressure, whatever their experience, and steadiness is developed over time — it is not a trait you either have or lack. And twelfth. Speaking up is good judgement, not failure. When the signs of not coping settle in and stay, talk to a trusted colleague, a mentor or a manager — and, if it is persistent or serious, to a healthcare professional, such as your G-P. Dealing with something early, while it is small, is the responsible and professional thing to do.
